did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a lightweight Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) optimized by a Harmonic Mean Optimizer (HMO) combined with a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier for automated skin cancer diagnosis. It also employs Monte Carlo Dropout for uncertainty estimation and Grad-CAM for visual explainability on dermoscopic images from the ACS and ISIC 2018 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' in the Jordan Journal of Applied Science. It presents original experimental work, including the development of a novel lightweight CNN-SVM framework optimized with HMO, training on the ACS dataset, validation on the ISIC 2018 dataset, and reporting of specific performance metrics (e.g., 96.1% accuracy) and robustness analyses.

title: Lightweight CNN-SVM Framework Optimized with HMO for Robust and Explainable Skin Cancer Diagnosis
brief_summary: This study proposes a lightweight, interpretable, and uncertainty-aware framework for automated skin cancer diagnosis using dermoscopic images. The system integrates a Harmonic Mean Optimizer (HMO)-tuned Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier, enhanced by Monte Carlo Dropout for uncertainty estimation and Grad-CAM for visual explainability. The model was trained on the American Cancer Society (ACS) dataset and validated without retraining on the ISIC 2018 dataset to assess cross-dataset generalization. On the ACS dataset, the framework achieved 96.1% accuracy, 96.8% sensitivity, and 94.7% specificity, while maintaining 94.8% accuracy on the ISIC 2018 dataset. Robustness was confirmed through testing under synthetic perturbations, where accuracy remained above 93.7%, and the model demonstrated strong discriminative capacity with an AUC of 0.987. The results indicate that the proposed HMO-CNN+SVM framework offers a reliable, efficient, and explainable solution suitable for deployment in resource-limited clinical settings.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on automated skin cancer detection, specifically highlighting melanoma as the primary target for screening. The model distinguishes between malignant (melanoma) and benign lesions.

The study performs binary classification distinguishing malignant lesions (melanoma) from benign lesions. Specific benign subtypes (e.g., nevus, seborrheic keratosis) are not explicitly listed as separate classes in the provided text, but are grouped under 'benign'.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of dermoscopic images into 'Cancerous' (melanoma) vs. 'Non-Cancerous' (benign) categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly describes a binary classification task. The output layer has 2 neurons for 'Cancerous vs. Non-Cancerous classification' (Chunk 11). The model distinguishes between malignant lesions (melanoma) and benign lesions. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as Accuracy (96.1%), Sensitivity (96.8%), Specificity (94.7%), PPV, NPV, and AUC (0.987) on the ACS and ISIC 2018 datasets (Chunks 1, 12, 15).

To report the uncertainty range for classification metrics (Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity) derived from the five-fold cross-validation results.

Listed in the evaluation protocol summary (Table 2) under 'Statistical Validity', implying their use for comparing results or validating significance, though specific p-values or test statistics are not detailed in the provided text.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in the performance metrics (e.g., accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) of the proposed HMO-CNN+SVM framework compared to baseline models or across cross-validation folds.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in the performance metrics of the proposed HMO-CNN+SVM framework compared to baseline models or across cross-validation folds.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper lists 't-tests' under 'Statistical Validity' in Table 2 as part of the evaluation protocol to ensure rigorous validation. However, specific p-values, test statistics, or explicit interpretations of significance levels are not reported in the provided text. The paper primarily relies on mean metrics with 95% confidence intervals and comparative benchmarking rather than explicit hypothesis test outcomes.

ACS Dataset (American Cancer Society).notes: The model maintained accuracy above 93.7% under all perturbation scenarios. Robustness scores remained over 97% across perturbations. The cumulative false negative rate was 1.5% (9/585) and false positive rate was low (12/321).

ISIC 2018 Dataset.notes: The model showed only a 1.3% drop in accuracy compared to the ACS dataset, confirming strong cross-dataset generalization. The framework sustained over 93.7% accuracy under strong distortions on this dataset as well.

training_details.uncertainty_estimation: Monte Carlo Dropout with 30 random forward passes during inference

classification_or_output_head.feature_selection: HMO-driven feature selection based on statistical, morphological, and textural descriptors (area, perimeter, contrast, homogeneity, entropy, eccentricity, invariant moments)

conclusions: The proposed HMO-CNN+SVM framework achieves high diagnostic accuracy (96.1% on ACS dataset) and strong cross-dataset generalization (94.8% on ISIC 2018 dataset without retraining), demonstrating resilience to data distribution shifts.
The framework exhibits robustness against synthetic perturbations (Gaussian noise, rotation, brightness variations), maintaining accuracy above 93.7% across all tested scenarios.

The model offers superior computational efficiency with only 3.1 million parameters compared to heavier benchmarks like AlexNet (~60M), VGG-16 (~138M), and ResNet-50 (~25.6M), making it suitable for resource-constrained and mobile health settings.

The integration of Grad-CAM provides visual explainability that aligns with expert-annotated lesion regions, while Monte Carlo Dropout enables effective uncertainty quantification (ECE < 3.5%), addressing key limitations of interpretability and reliability in current AI diagnostic tools.

The system is positioned as a reliable, scalable solution for teledermatology and community health initiatives in underdeveloped areas due to its balance of performance, interpretability, and low computational demand.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small American Cancer Society (ACS) dataset (n=906) for training and validation, which may limit the statistical power and generalizability of the results compared to larger, multi-center cohorts.

External validation was performed on the ISIC 2018 dataset without retraining; however, the specific class distribution and total sample size of the ISIC 2018 subset used for this validation are not explicitly reported, making it difficult to assess potential class imbalance in the external test set.

Robustness was evaluated using synthetic perturbations (Gaussian noise, rotation, brightness) rather than real-world clinical variations or adversarial attacks, which may overestimate the model's resilience to actual clinical imaging artifacts.

The paper claims 't-tests' were used for statistical validity but does not report specific p-values or test statistics for the comparisons between the proposed model and baselines, limiting the ability to verify statistical significance.

The model achieves high accuracy (96.1%) but with a low false negative rate (1.5%); while clinically desirable, the small number of false negatives (9 cases) in the ACS test set means the sensitivity estimate has a wide confidence interval and may be unstable.

The framework integrates an SVM classifier with HMO-optimized feature selection, which differs from standard end-to-end deep learning pipelines; the complexity of this hybrid approach and its computational overhead during inference are not fully detailed.

The study focuses on binary classification (melanoma vs. benign) and does not evaluate performance on specific benign subtypes (e.g., nevus, seborrheic keratosis), which are common sources of diagnostic confusion in clinical practice.

The authors claim the model is suitable for resource-limited settings due to its lightweight architecture (3.1M parameters), but do not provide empirical latency or memory usage metrics on actual edge devices or mobile hardware.
